channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘Country of Lords,’ by Kim Phillips-Fein
item.description: In a new book, the historian Kim Phillips-Fein chronicles a “shadow tradition” of anti-egalitarians who fought against America’s founding ideals.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: Country of Lords: Neo-Aristocrats, Social Darwinists, Tech Utopians, and the Long Fight Against Equality in America (Book)
item.media:credit: The New York Times; MPI/Getty Images
item.media:description: When the white supremacist historian Lothrop Stoddard challenged the sociologist W.E.B. Du Bois at a public debate over race and intelligence in 1929, the audience found Stoddard unpersuasive.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Unsayable,’ by Michael Cunningham
item.description: Blending autobiography with meditations on craft, “Unsayable” feels destined for the graduation gift bag.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Taylor
item.media:credit: Eric Ruby for The New York Times
item.media:description: Taking us from his earliest memory to the present-ish day, Cunningham’s memoir is not so much a straight line as a set of revolving doors.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Mortons,’ by Justine Larbalestier and Scott Westerfeld
item.description: In “The Mortons,” a young hit woman — a prodigy at killing — is tested by inherited secrets and her own morality.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: New Science Fiction About Truth and Lies in an Age of War
item.description: Three recent novels explore the use of deception by both invaders and the invaded.
item.dc:creator: Amal El-Mohtar

item.title: Book Review: ‘Bitchy!,’ by Roberta Gregory
item.description: A new compendium of Roberta Gregory’s comics does away with delicacy in the name of feminism.
item.dc:creator: Sam Thielman
item.category: Bitchy!: The Exasperating Existence of Midge McCracken (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Dad, Love, Me: A Memoir,’ by Matthew Quick
item.description: Matthew Quick’s memoir takes the form of a letter to his father as dementia slowly dismantles the man who once terrified him.
item.dc:creator: Tas Tobey

item.title: Book Review: ‘Cool Machine,’ by Colson Whitehead
item.description: The final installment of his Harlem crime trilogy is full of heists, cultural shifts and nostalgia for 1980s New York.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.media:credit: Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
item.media:description: Colson Whitehead details urban realities both grimy and plush in his acclaimed trilogy of crime novels.

item.title: The Scariest Place in New Jersey Set the Stage for Her Novel
item.description: Caitlin Mullen drew on eerie expeditions to the Pine Barrens for her new mystery novel, “Heather.”
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times

item.title: Want More of the ‘Odyssey’? Read These Books Next.
item.description: Informative histories, modern retellings and genre-bending homages will take you back to the long road to Ithaca.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: Book Review: ‘Famous Men,’ by Julie Buntin
item.description: Julie Buntin’s novel “Famous Men” complicates an old story with questions of ambition and consent.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Christensen

item.title: Golden Age Mysteries: A Starter Pack
item.description: Interested in these classic novels, but not sure where to start? Let our expert guide you.
item.dc:creator: Sadie Stein

item.title: Romance Novels Filled With Dazzling Fireworks
item.description: Our columnist on three of the year’s most anticipated books.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Waite

item.title: 75 Years Later, ‘The Catcher in the Rye’ Remains a Great American Novel
item.description: Sure, “Catcher in the Rye” has its detractors. But the influence of its wised-up yet winningly innocent hero keeps it among the Great American Novels.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: J.D. Salinger’s novel has sold some 80 million copies globally. The original cover features an illustration of a carousel horse.

item.title: They Wrote a Picture Book to Encourage Bipartisanship
item.description: Ellie Gottheimer and Larra Mullin, who met when their fathers were in Congress, make a case for bipartisanship in a picture book about dogs working together.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inspired by their fathers’ friendship, Ellie Gottheimer, left, and Larra Mullin wrote “Shmoo & Ozzie Go to Washington.”

item.title: Women Are Too Controlling, She Wrote. Her Message Still Resonates.
item.description: The message in “The Surrendered Wife,” the best-selling book by Laura Doyle, was provocative. Many women credit it with saving their marriages.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Yee and Isadora Kosofsky
item.media:description: Laura Doyle, author of “The Surrendered Wife" and several other books, alongside her husband, John Doyle.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Frenchmen,’ by Emily Eakin
item.description: In “The Frenchmen,” Emily Eakin traces the rise and fall of postmodernist philosophy, and the surprising ways its influence lingers.
item.dc:creator: Clancy Martin
item.category: The Frenchmen: Or, My Life in Theory (Book)
item.media:description: Michel Foucault at a protest in 1972, after a security guard killed an activist distributing fliers at a Renault factory. Jean-Paul Sartre stands in front of him.

item.title: 7 Other ‘Odyssey’ Movie Adaptations to Stream Now
item.description: For decades, filmmakers have been borrowing plot lines and metaphors from Homer’s epic for adventures set in different eras and realities.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: To Sell Homer’s ‘Odyssey,’ Book Publishers Get Creative
item.description: The market for the epic poem is as crowded as a certain Ithacan palace, and publishers have had to get crafty to attract readers to their editions.
item.dc:creator: MJ Franklin

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Lost Founder,’ by Jesse Wegman
item.description: In “The Lost Founder,” the journalist Jesse Wegman looks back at a largely forgotten and singular voice that helped make the United States what it is today.
item.dc:creator: Claire Rydell Arcenas
item.category: The Lost Founder: James Wilson and the Forgotten Fight for a People's Constitution (Book)
item.media:credit: Jeff Overs/BBC News, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The delegates at the Constitutional Convention of 1787 knew the world was watching and, for the most part, expecting them to fail.

item.title: Walt Odets, Who Delved Into the Minds of Gay Men, Dies at 79
item.description: A psychologist, he wrote during the AIDS crisis about survivor’s guilt and depression in his H.I.V.-negative patients.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: What to Know Before Seeing ‘The Odyssey’: Plot, Characters and More
item.description: Christopher Nolan’s adaptation of Homer’s epic poem has monster battles, a sorceress and dozens of thirsty suitors. Here’s a primer.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Matt Damon as Odysseus in the new movie.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Yellow Pine,’ by Claire Vaye Watkins
item.description: In Claire Vaye Watkins’s novel “Yellow Pine,” a single woman having an existential crisis decides now is the time to have a child.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Christensen

item.title: The Rediscovery of Country Original Gary Stewart
item.description: Gary Stewart spent a few years on top, but was largely forgotten after his tragic death in 2003. Now a new generation is rediscovering the onetime “king of honky-tonk.”
item.dc:creator: Bob Mehr

item.title: ‘Heartstopper Forever’: What to Know Before Seeing the Movie
item.description: The Netflix series and its feature-length finale are based on the graphic novels by Alice Oseman. Here’s a reminder of the events of the characters and where their stories left off.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: The Children’s Books of Seymour Chwast
item.description: A new collection of the famous graphic designer Seymour Chwast’s work for children “unearths a cache of lost treasures.”
item.dc:creator: Bruce Handy
item.category: The Picture Book Book: The Art of Seymour Chwast (Book)

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: Aging Out: An Exploration of Caregiving, Community, and How Americans Grow Old (Book)
A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
Catch the Devil: A True Story of Murder, Deception, and Injustice on the Gulf Coast (Book)
They Stole a City: Wilmington's White Supremacist Coup and the Families Who Live With Its Legacy (Book)
It Will Come Back to You: Collected Stories (Book)

item.title: The Only Thing Riskier Than Telling an Abortion Joke
item.description: For the comedian Alison Leiby, talking about her choice to be child-free is more polarizing to audiences than anything else.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.category: I'm a Lot: Surviving Myself and All the People I've Been (Book)
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Leiby’s new book, “I’m a Lot,” is a collection of funny, intimate essays that touch on everything from her reality TV addiction to chronic pain.

item.title: Book Review: ‘I Want You to Be Happy,’ by Jem Calder
item.description: Jem Calder’s novel, “I Want You to Be Happy,” follows the flailing situationship between two aspiring writers with a 12-year age gap.
item.dc:creator: Lizzy Harding
item.category: I Want You to Be Happy (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Journey to Nowhere,’ by Shiva Naipaul
item.description: In “Journey to Nowhere,” Shiva Naipaul reports on the aftermath of a cult’s disastrous turn away from idealism.
item.dc:creator: Anderson Tepper
item.category: Journey to Nowhere: A New World Tragedy (Book)
People's Temple of Disciples of Christ
item.media:credit: Stuart William MacGladrie/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Shiva Naipaul, while lesser known than his older brother, V.S., published a small but vital body of work.
